This is a cleaner way to do it.  BaseExceptions should generally not be caught.  The need to catch CancelledError and re-raise it is because it was an `Exception` in 3.7 and promoted to a `BaseExcetpion` only in 3.8.  So, for 3.7 we must make sure it isn't handled with the more general `Exception`.

Please try to launch the tests under python 2.7.

This is how I do it:
```
$ cd trash-cli
$ virtualenv env2 -p /usr/bin/python2
$ source env2/bin/activate
$ pip install -r requirements.txt -r requirements-dev.txt
$ rehash -r
$ pytest
```

The location of python2 (`/usr/bin/python2`) may be different in your computer, you may need to install it from your system package manager. If python2 is not available in you system/distribution you may want to use pyenv.
```
$ brew install pyenv # this is valid only if you use Homebrew, you can install pyenv from other means, see https://github.com/pyenv/pyenv for the best the best method.
$ pyenv install 2.7.18
....
Installed Python-2.7.18 to /Users/andrea/.pyenv/versions/2.7.18
```
The you can create your virtual environment with python2
$ virtualenv --python /Users/andrea/.pyenv/versions/2.7.18/bin/python2.7 env2
```

Thank you a lot for being so diligent and for being open to suggestions. I have a few minor comments still, please take a look at them.

> I just applied some basic boy-scout logic as you said that skorch identation was supposed to be 2 spaces (and it was 4 there).

This was a good idea. However, we use 4 spaces everywhere, but docstrings use 2 spaces for paragraphs.

> There was indeed an error, as I was storing in EarlyStopping the mutable net.module_.state_dict object without copying it, which would keep being modified.

Ah yes, that's a nasty error, I didn't catch it either.

There is one more thing that comes to my mind, sorry that I didn't think of it earlier: Given this implementation, when someone pickles the net, it would be twice as big because both the weights of the `module_` and the `EarlyStopping.best_model_weights_` would be stored. I wonder if that is a good thing. What do you think about not storing `EarlyStopping.best_model_weights_`?

In that case, one would need to override `__getstate__` from `EarlyStopping` and pop the `best_model_weights_`, probably replacing it with `None`. In `on_train_end`, there would need to be an extra check if `best_model_weights_ is not None` before loading it into the `state_dict`.

I'm trying to imagine if this could cause trouble in practice. Say someone trains a model with early stopping and afterwards pickles the whole model. If later on they decide to load the model and continue training, they would not need the `best_model_weights_` attribute because these weights are already equal to the model's current weights. Is there some other use case where this could matter?

Hi @Kirmas and thanks for the PR! I did a very brief review and added some comments inline. 

The mid-term plan (i.e., for the 0.6 release) for this library is to start moving device integrations to be more plugin-like and self-contained. Considering that this PR introduces a new directory as well as new utility data files, I'd like to see this PR as a step towards that direction. Therefore I suggest that instead of introducing a new `yeelight_specs` data directory, please create a new `yeelight` directory to hold all the auxiliary files.

In order to make the code reviews related to functional changes easier, please do not move `yeelight.py` into that directory. Otherwise checking which changes are just refactoring and which are functional is hard and time-consuming. After this PR gets merged to the master, a new PR should be created to perform the move.

I checked your PR. It adds some nice improvements to the notebook and in general I agree with them. There are just various small style issues. Unfortunately GitHub won't allow me to write comments next to the code of this PR. This is because images in the notebook have changed and that causes that the overall change is too for GitHub to display the code. So I'm just writing all my comments here:

- `import os` is redundant

- I don't particularly like how the 2nd cell, where users have to insert credentials, is written. (I also didn't like the previous version.) It is because it doesn't take into account that users could have their credentials saved in config.json or, in case of AWS credentials, even in `~/.aws/credentials` file. Instead it forces them to write credentials into the notebook. I recommend something like:

```python
# Insert your credentials here in case you don't already have them in config.json file:
SH_CLIENT_ID = ''
SH_CLIENT_SECRET = ''
AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID = ''
AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY = ''


if SH_CLIENT_ID and SH_CLIENT_SECRET:
    config.sh_client_id = SH_CLIENT_ID
    config.sh_client_secret = SH_CLIENT_SECRET
    
if not config.sh_client_id or not config.sh_client_secret:
    print("Warning! To use Sentinel Hub BYOC API, please provide the credentials (client ID and client secret).")
    
if AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID and AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY:
    config.aws_access_key_id = AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID
    config.aws_secret_access_key = AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY
```

I wouldn't even raise a warning if AWS credentials are not set because they can have them in `~/.aws/credentials` file.

- No need to define too many new variables if they will only be used in the next line. Instead of:
```python
created_collection_name = created_collection['name']
created_collection_id = created_collection['id']
print('name:', created_collection_name)
print('id:', created_collection_id)
```
you can just do
```python
print('name:', created_collection['name'])
print('id:', created_collection['id'])
```

- Instead of
```python
my_collection_using_next = next(collections_iterator)
my_collection_using_next
print('name:', my_collection_using_next['name'])
print('id:', my_collection_using_next['id'])
```
you can remove the redundant line:
```python
my_collection_using_next = next(collections_iterator)

print('name:', my_collection_using_next['name'])
print('id:', my_collection_using_next['id'])
```

- This note

"Note: next() will only show the first collection on its first execution, the second collection for its second execution, and so on. if you already have collections the output above won’t necessarily show the one we just created." 

mostly just describes how an iterator and `next` function work. Perhaps the part worth mentioning is only that `collections_iterator` won't necessarily return collections in the same order they were created.

- You have: "Below we’re going to rename the ‘byoc-tutorial’ collection to ‘byoc-tutorial-renamed’:" but then you rename `'new collection'` into `'renamed new collection'`.

- Typo in "Trying to access this collection now will fali." -> "fail"

- In sentence:

"users can have a look at the documentation or use the BYOC tool that will take care of creating a collection and ingesting the tiles for you."

there used to be links to documentation but there are not anymore there. Any reason for removing them?

- Instead of "Sentinel-Hub" it should be "Sentinel Hub" everywhere. I see that already the previous version had this mistake.

- Where you have a note with bullet points:

```
**Note:**
- The most important field of the tile is its `path` on an s3 bucket. ...
```
make sure to add an empty line:

```
**Note:**

- The most important field of the tile is its `path` on an s3 bucket. ...
```
otherwise it won't parse into documentation correctly.

- In function signature:

```python
list_objects_path(bucket, aws_access_key_id, aws_secret_access_key, y=None, m=None, d=None):
```
I recommend renaming `y`, `m`, `d` into something more descriptive, e.g. `year_count`, `month_count`, `day_count`. Single-letter names can here be even a bit misleading as one would think you have to specify current year, month and day. It also doesn't make sense to have default values `None` for them as such values wouldn't work. I also recommend simply passing `config` object instead of both parameters for AWS credentials. So the signature could be:

```python
def list_objects_path(bucket, year_count, month_count, day_count, config):
```

- Line:
```python
tiles_path = list_objects_path('sentinel-s2-l2a-mosaic-120', config.aws_access_key_id, config.aws_secret_access_key, y=1, m=1, d=1)
```
is quite long, so I recommend splitting it into
```python
tiles_path = list_objects_path(
    'sentinel-s2-l2a-mosaic-120',
    config.aws_access_key_id,
    config.aws_secret_access_key,
    y=1,
    m=1,
    d=1
)
```

- Indentation in:
```python
    byoc_tile = ByocTile(path=f'{tile}(BAND).tif', 
                         sensing_time=dt.datetime(int(tile.split('/')[0]), 
                                                  int(tile.split('/')[1]), 
                                                  int(tile.split('/')[2]), 
                                                  0, 
                                                  0, 
                                                  0)
                        )
```
is a bit too much. I suggest changing to something like:

```python
year, month, day = tile.split('/')[:3]
byoc_tile = ByocTile(
    path=f'{tile}(BAND).tif', 
    sensing_time=dt.datetime(int(year), int(month), int(day))
)
```
- You can simplify sentence "To request one specific tile in the collection, you can do it by passing the collection id and the tile id to the `get_tile` method." into "To request a specific tile you can pass a collection id and a tile id to the `get_tile` method."

- There are `4` cases in the code where you write something like:

```python
[x['path'] for x in tiles if x['status']=='FAILED']
```
I recommend choosing a better name for variable `x` and also mind the spaces around `==` operator:
```python
[tile['path'] for tile in tiles if tile['status'] == 'FAILED']
```

- This was here from before, but line:

```python
tiles_gdf = gpd.GeoDataFrame(tiles_for_visualized, geometry=[t.cover_geometry.transform(CRS.WGS84).geometry for t in tiles_for_visualized], crs='epsg:4326')
```
is also a bit too long so I recommend splitting it:

```python
tiles_gdf = gpd.GeoDataFrame(
    tiles_for_visualized,
    geometry=[tile.cover_geometry.transform(CRS.WGS84).geometry for tile in tiles_for_visualized],
    crs='epsg:4326'
)
```

- In the sentence " ... the ingested tiles are 100 tiles from the pull out from the Sentinel-2 L2A 120m Mosaic ..." I think you can remove "from the pull out " part as it is a bit confusing.

- In the following there is a typo and a missing empty line which causes bad parsing into documentation:

```
Updating and deleting a tile follow the same logic as updating/deleting a collection:
- To updatea tile:
```

I recommend:

```
Updating and deleting a tile follow the same logic as updating/deleting a collection.

- To update a tile:
```

- Typo in "Alternatively using dataclasee:"

- Another mistake which we already had in the previous version: The following has too much indentation:

```python
request = SentinelHubRequest(
        evalscript=false_color_evalscript,
        input_data=[
            SentinelHubRequest.input_data(
                data_collection=data_collection,
                time_interval=tile_time
            )
        ],
        responses=[
            SentinelHubRequest.output_response('default', MimeType.PNG)
        ],
        bbox=caspian_sea_bbox,
        size=bbox_to_dimensions(caspian_sea_bbox, 100),
        config=config
    )
```
it should be:

```python
request = SentinelHubRequest(
    evalscript=false_color_evalscript,
    input_data=[
        SentinelHubRequest.input_data(
            data_collection=data_collection,
            time_interval=tile_time
        )
    ],

        SentinelHubRequest.output_response('default', MimeType.PNG)
    ],
    bbox=caspian_sea_bbox,

```

FWIW the idea for the initial plugin implementation was to stay close to the previous `conda-*` API and have a similar behavior and specifically not provide an `argparse` specific plugin hook.

That was a design decision to reduce the effort needed for existing packages extending conda with `conda-*` scripts to have a transition path and gain some experience before we support more advanced techniques like supporting click etc.

I've left a few comments and think this still has value FWIW, e.g. we could move this into an own `ArgparseCondaSubcommand` class that could be an “advanced” option for plugins with more complex needs. That said, generally speaking, I would caution us to leaning into `argparse` given its obvious API problems. I'd rather have us work towards supporting tools like [click](https://click.palletsprojects.com/en/8.1.x/) or [typer](https://typer.tiangolo.com/).

Typically code-changes that modify the API, [should be discussed](https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md#tldr) on a separate issue prior opening the PR. Unfortunately we can't merge this because:
- The attribute name used here is not consistent with the equivalents of other models

According to the ST7735 documentation, a reset is triggered if RESX is held low longer than 5us and can take up to 120ms to complete depending on the power state of the display (see section 9.16 in [ST7735 data sheet](https://www.displayfuture.com/Display/datasheet/controller/ST7735.pdf))  If this were a display specific setting (as opposed to the interface), I'd recommend making the default something like 150ms.  Since it is not, this seems like a reasonable approach.  

FYI, it is possible that the problem is not how long you held the pin low as I doubt that you can toggle the pin from high-low-high in under 5us using RPi.GPIO. I suspect instead it was beginning to transmit data before the reset was completed.  This issue of certain commands (like reset) taking longer than other commands is a problem I was attempting to solve by introducing an optional exec_time in parallel_device so that depending on the command being issued, an appropriate delay to wait for the command to finish could be specified.  Just something to think about...

One other item, if you are going to release another version of `luma.core`, I'd like to introduce a change to the exception handling of `bitmap_font` to eliminate the dependency on `Pillow` >=7.  I'll put a PR up for that shortly.  Happy to pull it back if you want to take a different direction.

IMO, it's a bit sketchy to run the existing autodetect logic by default, since it's effectively grepping ps aux output. The two issues I see are:
1. It's possible for there to be a false positive.
2. If a cluster died, then it wouldn't autodetect it correctly (false negative).

How about the following instead?
- `ray start` writes the created $RAY_ADDRESS to `/tmp/ray/default_ray_address`
- we check `/tmp/ray/default_ray_address` if $RAY_ADDRESS isn't defined
- `ray stop` deletes $RAY_ADDRESS

In addition to the review, I verified that the two implementations for boxes and losses return the same result by running:
```python
import torch

from torchvision.ops.boxes import distance_box_iou
from torchvision.ops.diou_loss import distance_box_iou_loss

def random_box(canvas_size):
    x1y1 = torch.rand((1, 2)) * canvas_size
    wh = torch.rand((1, 2)) * canvas_size
    x2y2 = (x1y1 + wh).clamp(0, canvas_size)
    return torch.cat((x1y1, x2y2), axis=1)


canvas_size = 1000


for _ in range(10000):
    box1 = random_box(canvas_size)
    box2 = random_box(canvas_size)

    v1 = 1 - distance_box_iou(box1, box2)
    v2 = distance_box_iou_loss(box1, box2)

    torch.testing.assert_close(v1[0], v2, rtol=0, atol=1e-6)
```

High-level comments:

- Let's try to avoid the `_FixedDirCheckpoint` completely
- Instead, let's only handle the case from object ref --> to directory
- We can do this in the regular `Checkpoint`
- In this case, we can construct a canonical temp file name from the object ref ID

IMO this makes sense as data is immutable in the object store, so we can guarantee that an object ref always contains the same data. We can't do the same for directories (and it doesn't make much sense as it would be dir to dir), and doing it for dicts could be a bit more expensive (if we e.g. calculate a hash of a large dict checkpoint). 

If I see it correctly, handling object ref alone will suffice the use case with the batch predictor, correct?

Great PR so far, I have added minor comments inline.

Also, I think it is worth to put additional python functions to compute code_challenge_value and code_verifier. The oauthlib framework still needs to be open to any custom values, but at the same time, by default, it should prepare the ground for the implementor. For example, see the RFC:

```
   The client first creates a code verifier, "code_verifier", for each
   OAuth 2.0 [RFC6749] Authorization Request, in the following manner:

   code_verifier = high-entropy cryptographic random STRING using the
   unreserved characters [A-Z] / [a-z] / [0-9] / "-" / "." / "_" / "~"
   from Section 2.3 of [RFC3986], with a minimum length of 43 characters
   and a maximum length of 128 characters.

   ABNF for "code_verifier" is as follows.

   code-verifier = 43*128unreserved
   unreserved = ALPHA / DIGIT / "-" / "." / "_" / "~"
   ALPHA = %x41-5A / %x61-7A
   DIGIT = %x30-39

   NOTE: The code verifier SHOULD have enough entropy to make it
   impractical to guess the value.  It is RECOMMENDED that the output of
   a suitable random number generator be used to create a 32-octet
   sequence.  The octet sequence is then base64url-encoded to produce a
   43-octet URL safe string to use as the code verifier.
```

and
```
   The client then creates a code challenge derived from the code
   verifier by using one of the following transformations on the code
   verifier:

   plain
      code_challenge = code_verifier

   S256
      code_challenge = BASE64URL-ENCODE(SHA256(ASCII(code_verifier)))

   If the client is capable of using "S256", it MUST use "S256", as
   "S256" is Mandatory To Implement (MTI) on the server.  Clients are
   permitted to use "plain" only if they cannot support "S256" for some
   technical reason and know via out-of-band configuration that the
   server supports "plain".
```

By doing this in oauthlib side, it will prevent implementor to do mistake during the implementation and will be very easy to implement it right.
Thoughts?

I noticed that you made changes to `field.py` to only allow deprecation on nullable fields. According to the Spec, this constraint only applies to input fields. 
Look at the following example from spec:

```
type ExampleType {
  invalidField(
    newArg: String
    oldArg: String! @deprecated(reason: "Use `newArg`.")
  ): String
}
```
With input fields, the restriction makes sense, as we don't have any alternative to passing a value to the required `oldField`, although `oldField` is deprecated. For input fields, the alternative is to not query the field anymore.

I'm fine with the added functionality, but I want to note the following caveats:

- Users can still get `MemcachedUnexpectedCloseError`, even with this change. This is a distributed system, so it will be very easy for the timing to work out such that the connection looks "live" on the client side, but has become "idle" on the server side.
- Users still need to expect and handle `MemcacheUnexpectedCloseError`. I'm starting to think, after recent discussions, that we need to provide more guidance for how to do this. There is no way to guarantee that you will never get this exception.

With those caveats, I think this can eliminate some spurious cases of `MemcacheUnexpectedCloseError`, which should make normal error handling a little more stream-lined.

This looks good overall. I'll defer to you on performance changes, I trust that you and Bradley have tested sufficiently. Regarding the ManagedArray discussion, the thing to keep in mind is that conceptually `ManagedArray` is really a low-level RAII wrapper around a pointer to a contiguous memory buffer whose ownership can be transferred. `std::vector` should be used more like a list, whereas `ManagedArray` should be like a numpy array. Since `l` values are arbitrary and not necessarily sequential, a `std::vector<ManagedArray>` makes sense. The expectation is that Python users acquiring `ManagedArray` variables expect that those variables should be zero-copy but remain unchanged if a recompute is triggered. Since it looks like you're replacing all the `ManagedArray` data in the vector on recompute, I think you'll be fine. I'll double-check this on my final pass though since I was a bit cursory with checking that this time around.

The colon may give some trouble to users that either copy the files to Windows, or that run `cwltool` on Windows linux subsystem. I think in Windows `:` is reserved for file system drive names, and cannot (or could not last I checked) be used in file names, unless we escape it.

I had a quick test manually, and spaces are not allowed :heavy_check_mark: , commas accepted :heavy_check_mark: , colon accepted :heavy_check_mark: . Accents in words used in Portuguese (ã, é, etc) passed the regex too :heavy_check_mark: .Then tested with a couple Japanese characters :heavy_check_mark: , and found an issue with common symbols used in Japanese, but not sure if important.

The `\w` in the regex appears to match words in Japanese in any of its three writing systems (hiragana, katakana, kanji), not sure about other Chinese characters, but shouldn't be a problem :heavy_check_mark: 

Other Japanese [symbols](http://www.localizingjapan.com/blog/2012/01/20/regular-expressions-for-japanese-text/) that are valid in file names, and are not spaces, are not supported, such as the parentheses "「」" or the Japanese version for comma/dot "、。". 

These symbols can be used in normal file names in Windows/Linux, but I think we cannot guarantee every symbol of every language will be supported, so that should be fine I think.

As per DM discussions - this snapshot mutation approach is very concerning due to it changing the immutable nature of the snapshot objects. It is very likely that we will leverage the immutable nature of snapshots for things like caching in the future and accounting for this type of complexity would make that very difficult. 

As was called out in the summary, an approach roughly like 

> Is there some other mutable object we need to create that can store cross-repo dependencies that can be referenced from the graphql layer?

is much more appealing. `PipelineIndex` is an example of an object that layers over immutable snapshots to support efficient access patterns in host processes. 

I also think it is worth revisiting how asset nodes are modeled at the graphql layer to account for this problem. It's possible that something like having separate top level nodes and edges/dependencies information instead of just top level list of nodes where the nodes know their edges/deps could be easier to merge across multiple repositories.

I've played with the example and looked through most of the code and I'm super impressed! Great job Lisa! I actually wouldn't change very much, just the default behaviour for old users and introduce a very conservative suggestion for the ``global_constraints``.

## Naming schemes

I agree with your naming: we need ``investment_periods`` to distinguish from ``snapshot_periods`` that run through the year. Old PyPSA "overnight" behaviour is often called "multi-period" optimisation, but we need to make clear that we also do multiple investment periods here.

## Default behaviour

The guiding principle is that old users shouldn't have to make any big changes, and that there should be no surprises.

I suggest that n.snapshots stays the same, and can be either MultiIndex or Index.

All PyPSA functions should by default treat MultiIndex snapshots just the same as Index snapshots. This is the case for old PyPSA ``n.lopf(pyomo=False)``, but ``n.lopf(pyomo=True)``, ``n.lpf()`` and ``n.pf()`` all break when using MultiIndex -we should fix this in future releases (but not now).

The multi-period optimisation is the only one to treat MultiIndex differently, and must be activated with:

``n.lopf(pyomo=False, multi_investment_period=True)``

When this is activated, it checks that ``n.snapshots`` is MultiIndex, that level 0 is integer, and that the integers are increasing. It also checks that all investment periods appear in the index of ``n.investment_period_weightings``.

If ``n.lopf(pyomo=True, multi_investment_period=True)`` is called, an error is thrown.

### Snapshot weightings

This is a parallel change to the investment periods. In PyPSA Version 0.18.0 we will replace the pd.Series ``n.snapshot_weightings`` with a pd.DataFrame with three columns ``[objective_weightings, generator_weightings, store_weightings]``. This separates out the three current use cases of weightings in the LOPF:

- ``objective_weightings``: for operational costs in the objective function
- ``store_weightings``: for elapsed time in the filling of ``Store`` and ``StorageUnit`` components
- ``generator_weightings``: for the contribution of generators to ``GlobalConstraint`` components for e.g. CO2 budgets

Example use cases:

- case 1 3-hourly sampling for full year: ``objective_weighting = generator_weighting = store_weighting = 3``.
- case hourly sampling for 10 days to represent full year with cyclic storage: ``objective weighting = generator_weighting = 52. , 
store_weighting = 1.``

BUT this case fails if e.g. a ``Store`` represents non-cyclic depletion of e.g. biomass or some other finite resource, in which case you want ``store_weighting = 52``, like ``generator_weighting``. This interface need all storage doing short-term cycling, or all storage non-cyclic (depleting a finite resource). We should fix this when we implement Kotzur's seasonal storage scheme.

### Investment period weightings

I think the way Lisa implemented this looks good, maybe change the names of weightings to be similar to the ``snapshot_weightings``?

I also suggest not to have ``n.investment_periods``, but track these only via level 0 of ``n.snapshots``.

### Behaviour when an empty network is generated

If you call ``n = pypsa.Network()`` by default will be created:

- ``n.snapshots = pd.Index(["now"])``
- ``n.snapshot_weightings = pd.DataFrame(1.,1.,1.,index=["now"],
                                     columns=["objective_weightings","store_weightings","generator_weightings"])``
- ``n.investment_period_weightings = pd.DataFrame(columns=["objective_weightings","store_weightings","generator_weightings"])``

### Behaviour when a network from PyPSA Version < 0.18.0 is imported

Create new empty ``n.investment_period_weightings``.

The values of pd.Series ``n.snapshot_weightings`` are copied across all three values of the new pd.DataFrame ``n.snapshot_weightings``.

## Global constraints for ``p_nom_max`` at each bus, carrier and investment_period

I suggest to keep these all in ``n.global_constraints`` since they are global in the sense of affecting multiple components at once. We need to add to the ``GlobalConstraint`` table attributes ``bus``, ``carrier`` and ``investment_period`` and type ``multi_investment_period_p_nom_max`` or similar.

Yes, this will create lots of global constraints, but introducing e.g. ``n.global_constraints_p`` doesn't help since you need 3-d information.

This way is minimally invasive.

## Treatment of sub_networks

I think the current way is good. The sub_networks are generated as needed, and the last one to be generated is stored. There is no need to store them from all investment periods, i.e. no need for ``n.sub_networks_p``.

The sub_network is anyway more of a hidden variable.

## Storage behaviour between investment periods

Did I understand correctly that cyclic storage is cyclic within each investment period? This behaviour would be fine.

If the storage is not cyclic, is energy transfered between investment periods? Probably this is not such a good idea for e.g. depletable biomass. But maybe we should model depletable biomass as a generator, rather than as a store that starts full. Then the generator can fill the storage at the start of each year?

## Check objective function

Since the objective function is quite complicated now, please check for some non-trivial examples that the Gurobi objective function is also what you calculated from the results. This should help to find bugs.

## Coding style

Try to avoid abbreviations like ``inv_p`` and ``sns`` in API functions like ``network.determine_network_topology``. Here we should be explicit for understandability. You can abbreviate inside functions, that's fine.

Also try to avoid committing code very specific to your own setup, like paths to libraries.

> > Also, how do you include it in a `sdist` if it's outside?
> 
> This is the interesting part, if you review my code changes and description in the issue. It isn't copying the file into the project directory, it is copy the content and put in some place, in this case in the `PKG-INFO` file.


I think you may have missed the point. If your project loads its long_description from `../README`, then you build an sdist and ship that off to PyPI, and then I download the sdist and try to build it, it will fail to build because ../README doesn't exist.

What's worse, though, is if you use `long_description = file:/etc/passwd`, and then you build an sdist and ship that off to your colleague who builds a wheel on their build server and installs that to their applications, now `importlib.metadata.metadata('your-package')['Description']` will have the contents of `/etc/passwd` for the build server.

I'm sorry, but it's not about security, but it's about having hermetic packages whose sources are transportable.

I think you may wish to update the error message for clarity, but I recommend to do that in a separate PR.

1) I think this approach makes sense. I was going to say "it's a bummer this doesn't handle manual python_requirement targets", but we already handle that! Changing a BUILD file results in all its targets conservatively claiming to have been changed

2) We also would want to add this to poetry_requirements and pipenv_requirements. This is not blocking, but would be really nice to have.

3) It's important we don't create multiple targets for the same lockfile, which I think will result in an error. For example, if you have 2+ python_requirements target generators. This is blocking :/ Fixing is tough because we can't consult `AllTargets`, which requires these target generators to run to be evaluated. Idea: improve our target generation code to allow multiple generators to generate the same duplicate target, as long as it is identical.
  * Wait, actually, there is no risk for collisions. Each generated target is namespace by its target generator, e.g. `foo.lock:reqs` and `foo.lock:reqs2`. It's really weird to have multiple targets for the same lockfile...but not unsafe.

Sorry for the large number of comments. Took me half-way through before I had most of the details here figured out.

The main theme concludes to:
- I think that we want to swap the order of declaring defaults so we can do
```python
default_requirements = ["proj>=X.Y,<Z", "other", "stuff"]
default_version = default_requirements[0]
default_extra_requirements = default_requirements[1:]
```
Then we are in a good position to remove `default_version` and `default_extra_requirements`.

- I take it we employ a "default policy" of floating versions on a single major. Makes sense to me. Exceptions are to be expected, but when we find them, I think we would do well to document them.
 
- This is a large enough change already, so this may be for another PR. This line struck out at me:
```python 
 default_interpreter_constraints = ["CPython>=3.7,<4"]
```

At the very least we may want to move our "default default_interpreter_constraints" value to a CONST for re-use to DRY this up a bit.

The only broad/overarching thing that I think we need to address is `ToStr`. I have some inline comments on it, but, in short, I think `object` and most (all?) primitive types satisfy `ToStr`, which makes it very similar to `Any`. e.g.
```python
>>> import typing as t
>>> class ToStr(t.Protocol):
...     def __str__(self) -> str: ...
...
>>> rt = t.runtime_checkable(ToStr)
>>> x = object()
>>> isinstance(x, rt)
True
>>> def foo(): return 1
...
>>> isinstance(foo, rt)
True
>>> y = (1, 1)
>>> isinstance(y, rt)
True
```

So I think we may need to rethink that approach. For some cases, `Any` may actually be appropriate. And in others, we may want to define a smaller `Union` type, like `ID_PARAM_TYPE`.
